one_line_takeaway: Claw-Eval shows that grounding agent grading in independent trajectory evidence (traces + audit logs + snapshots) and a multiplicative safety gate catches nearly half the safety violations a transcript-only LLM judge misses, and that reliability (Pass^k) collapses under benign tool failures even when peak capability (Pass@k) looks stable.
key_finding: Trajectory-opaque grading is systematically unreliable: the vanilla LLM judge missed 12/27 (44%) of safety violations and 15/118 (13%) of robustness issues that the hybrid pipeline caught, because it cannot reliably apply deterministic parameter-level rules from text and sometimes rationalizes the agent's behavior. Separately, capability does not imply consistency: under error injection Pass@3 stays roughly flat while Pass^3 drops by up to 24 percentage points (Claude-Opus-4.6 most resilient, retaining 56.5% Pass^3 at rate 0.6; Pass@3-Pass^3 gap widens monotonically for every model). In multi-turn dialogue, question precision explains 76% of Pass^3 variance (r=0.87) whereas conversation length explains under 1% (r=0.07, R2<0.01). Rankings are strongly multi-dimensional: no single model leads across task groups or across the three multimodal domains.
core_contribution: Claw-Eval is an end-to-end evaluation suite for LLM autonomous agents built to close three gaps the authors identify in prior benchmarks (G1 trajectory-opaque grading, G2 underspecified safety/robustness, G3 narrow task coverage). Its own contributions are: (1) an auditable execution pipeline with a three-phase lifecycle (Setup, Execution, Judge) separated by a 'temporal firewall' so grading artifacts are never visible to the agent during solving, producing three independent evidence channels (execution traces, service-side audit logs, post-execution environment snapshots); (2) a 300-task, 9-category, 3-group benchmark (General 161, Multimodal 101, Multi-turn Dialogue 38) under a single declarative schema, decomposed into 2,159 human-verified rubric items (avg 7.2/task); (3) an integrated multi-dimensional scoring protocol over Completion, Safety, and Robustness where Safety acts as a multiplicative gate (score = s_safety x (alpha*s_completion + beta*s_robustness), alpha+beta=1) and Robustness is measured via controlled tool-error injection; and (4) a multi-trial protocol reporting Average Score, Pass@k (capability ceiling) and Pass^k (reliability floor). The security-relevant novelty vs. cited safety benchmarks (ToolEmu, R-Judge, Agent-SafetyBench, MobileRisk-Live, tau-bench) is embedding safety constraints inside ordinary workflow tasks and grading them deterministically from audit-log/parameter evidence rather than via risk-awareness-over-traces or standalone red-teaming.
threat_model: The paper's implicit threat model is unsafe or unfaithful agent behavior during otherwise-normal task execution. Two concrete concerns: (a) an agent taking a policy-violating action (e.g., an unsafe tool call with forbidden parameters) while still completing the task, which completion-only scoring would reward; the multiplicative safety gate is designed so a violation cannot be offset by high completion. (b) Trajectory-opaque / output-only grading being vulnerable to reward hacking and fabricated intermediate steps, letting shortcut or unsafe behavior pass undetected. The paper does NOT model an active adversary attacking the agent (no prompt injection, jailbreak, or malicious environment); the robustness axis injects only benign infrastructure faults (HTTP 429/500, latency spikes), and the authors explicitly defer semantic-level and adversarial perturbations to future work. So the frame is agent-safety/reliability auditing, not attacker-vs-defender security.
limitations: Author-stated: (1) robustness perturbations cover only infrastructure-level transient faults (HTTP 429/500, latency), not semantic or adversarial perturbations (deferred to future work) -- so the 'robustness' claim is narrow. (2) Open-ended rubric items depend on LLM judges (95% exact agreement with humans per Appendix F.3, but still an approximation that may not generalize). (3) Benchmark-longevity / contamination risk, mitigated only partially by trajectory evidence and deterministic gates. Reviewer-observed: the safety-violation base rate is tiny (27 flagged tasks total across models; the headline 44% is 12 of 27), so the miss-rate percentages rest on small counts. Multi-turn has only 38 tasks (STEM 10, Social 13, Business 15), limiting statistical power for the r=0.87 claim across 13 points. The judge for Multi-turn is Claude-Opus-4.6, which is also a top-scoring subject, a potential self-preference concern the paper does not fully address. Error-injection sweep uses only 3 models.

datasets_used: Claw-Eval benchmark (this paper): 300 human-verified tasks, 9 categories, 2,159 rubric items (avg 7.2/task)
Multimodal group: 101 tasks (Video 53, Doc & Image 22, Code 26)
Multi-turn Dialogue group: 38 tasks (STEM 10, Social Science 13, Business 15)
quantitative_results: Vanilla LLM judge missed 12/27 (44%) safety violations and 15/118 (13%) robustness issues vs. the hybrid pipeline (5 models, 2,000+ traces)
Overall Pass^3 leader: Claude-Opus-4.6 at 70.4%; Overall Score leader: Claude-Sonnet-4.6 at 81.4%; Overall Pass@3 leader: Sonnet-4.6/Gemini-3.1-Pro at 82.9%
General Pass^3: Opus-4.6 70.8%; Multi-turn Pass^3: Opus-4.6 68.4%; Gemini-3.1-Pro 2nd Multi-turn (65.8%) but 7th General (55.9%)
Multimodal is much harder: highest Pass^3 only 25.7% (GPT-5.4), vs 70.8% Opus-4.6 on General
Per-domain Multimodal Pass^3 leaders differ: Video 15.4% (Opus/Sonnet-4.6), Doc & Image 54.5% (GPT-5.4), Code 33.3% (MiMo-V2-Omni)
Aggregate multimodal Pass^3: Video 10.7%, Doc & Image 32.3%, Code 23.9%; consistency ratio rho=Pass^3/Pass@3: Video 0.37, Code 0.48, Doc & Image 0.53
Error injection 0.0->0.6: Pass^3 drops up to 24 pp while Pass@3 near-flat; Opus-4.6 retains 56.5% Pass^3 at rate 0.6, gap 9.9%->20.5%
Multi-turn: question precision r=0.87 (R2=0.76) vs round count r=0.07 (R2<0.01); models average 3-5 rounds, Pass^3 spans 15.8%-68.4%
General difficulty degradation (Opus-4.6 Pass^3): Easy 74.7 -> Hard 65.1; worst model Nemotron-3-Super Easy 14.1 -> Hard 0.0
LLM judges achieve 95% exact agreement with human scoring (Appendix F.3); median trial-to-trial sigma=1.2% on General
Weakest overall model: Nemotron-3-Super, Overall Pass^3 5.5%
baselines_compared: Vanilla single-call LLM judge (Gemini-3-Flash, transcript + grader source, no audit logs/snapshots) -- the methodological baseline against the hybrid grading pipeline

14 evaluated models: Claude-Opus-4.6, Claude-Sonnet-4.6, GPT-5.4, Gemini-3.1-Pro, Gemini-3-Flash, Qwen3.5-397B-A17B, MiMo-V2-Pro, MiMo-V2-Omni, GLM-5-Turbo, GLM-5V-Turbo, DeepSeek-V3.2, MiniMax-M2.7, Kimi-K2.5, Nemotron-3-Super
Prior benchmarks positioned against in Table 1 (related work, not re-run): AgentBench, GAIA, tau-bench, SWE-bench, WebArena, VisualWebArena, OSWorld, ToolBench, Terminal-Bench, PinchBench
